items.title: 21H.102 American History Since 1865
items.source_text: The course surveys United States history from the Civil War's end through the present. Students examine both secondary accounts and primary documents to understand major transformations in American development. Key topics include westward expansion conflicts, immigration and industrialization, U.S. global emergence, domestic and international Cold War dynamics, Civil Rights and social movements, and the digital age transition.

items.title: 21H.009 The World: 1400-Present
items.source_text: The course surveys 'the economic and political evolution of societies and cultures from 1400 to the present,' examining increasing global interaction, Western Europe's rise, and divergence between regions. It explores 'evolving relationships, including human beings and their physical environment; religious and political systems; and sub-groups within communities, sorted by race, class, and gender,' using diverse source materials including architectural and visual evidence.

items.title: 21L.002X Foundations of World Culture II: World Literatures and Texts
items.source_text: The course examines 'foundational texts of human culture, focusing on early modernity until the recent past.' Many readings challenge established traditions regarding women's status, colonialism, religion, and monarchical rule. Primary texts span Chinese literature (Cao Xuegin), philosophical works (Kant, Nietzsche, Mill), novels addressing colonialism and gender (Conrad, Salih, Kincaid), plus theoretical texts by Freud, Said, and Foucault.

items.title: 21L.003 Introduction to Fiction
topics: narrative voice and point of view
items.source_text: This course 'investigates the uses and boundaries of fiction in a range of novels and narrative styles — traditional and innovative, western and nonwestern,' exploring 'questions about the pleasures and meanings of verbal texts in different cultures, times, and forms.' Students are directed to a close reading guide addressing 'Why do we do close reading in literary study?' as a foundational approach framing the entire curriculum.

items.title: 24.00 Problems of Philosophy
topics: theism and the problem of evil
social construction of race and gender
the absurd and meaning of life
items.source_text: The syllabus describes the course as an introduction to 'problems in ethics, metaphysics, theory of knowledge, and philosophy of logic, language, and science,' with 'emphasis on examination and evaluation of proposed solutions to the problems.' Central questions include 'Can we prove the existence or nonexistence of a God?', 'What is the place of our consciousness in the physical world?', and 'Are there objective facts about what's good and bad?'

items.title: 24.118 Paradox and Infinity
items.source_text: The course presents 'highlights of the more technical side of philosophy' by examining 'a cluster of puzzles, paradoxes, and intellectual wonders — from the higher infinite to Gödel's Theorem' and their philosophical implications. Session topics progress from Infinite Cardinalities (Ch. 1) through The Higher Infinite (Ch. 2), Omega-Sequence Paradoxes (Ch. 3), Time Travel (Ch. 4), Newcomb's Problem (Ch. 5), Probability (Ch. 6), Non-Measurable Sets (Ch. 7), the Banach-Tarski Theorem (Ch. 8), Computability (Ch. 9), and Gödel's Theorem (Ch. 10).
